description: Pre-computed analysis + structural tips for the 15 most common textile compositions.

entries.microplastics: Each wash releases approximately 700,000 plastic microfibers that end up in the ocean and food chain.
entries.skinImpact: Non-breathable, traps heat and sweat. Can cause irritation, persistent odors and skin chafing.
entries.envImpact: Derived from crude oil, not biodegradable. Takes 200+ years to decompose in landfill.
entries.durability: 2–5 years with normal use. Resists washing but accumulates odors over time.
entries.summary: Wearable plastic: releases microfibers every wash and never truly decomposes.

tips.title: Use a Guppyfriend Bag
tips.body: Wash this garment inside an anti-microplastic laundry bag (Guppyfriend or Cora Ball). It captures up to 99% of fibers before they reach the sea.

tips.title: Cold wash, less often
tips.body: Washing at 30°C releases fewer microfibers than hot cycles. Only wash when truly necessary — polyester doesn't breathe but gets dirty less than natural fabrics.

tips.title: Look for OEKO-TEX certification
tips.body: For your next purchase, look for OEKO-TEX Standard 100 certified garments: guarantees no harmful substances. Even better: choose GOTS organic cotton or TENCEL instead of polyester.

entries.microplastics: The 65% polyester releases approximately 450,000 microfibers per wash — less than 100% synthetic, but still significant.
entries.skinImpact: Cotton improves breathability, but the synthetic component reduces freshness. Acceptable for daily use.
entries.envImpact: The blend makes recycling impossible: neither polyester nor cotton can be recovered separately.
entries.durability: 3–6 years. The blend is stronger than cotton alone but less pleasant over time.
entries.summary: A mass-market compromise: cheaper to produce, but unrecyclable and still polluting.

tips.title: Use a laundry bag
tips.body: 65% polyester releases a significant amount of microfibers. A Guppyfriend Bag reduces release by up to 99% — a one-time investment that lasts years.

tips.title: Not recyclable — don't bin it
tips.body: Cotton-polyester blends cannot be industrially separated. When you no longer use it, donate it or bring it to a textile collection point — not the trash.

tips.title: Next time: 100% organic cotton
tips.body: Look for garments with at least 80% natural origin. GOTS certification guarantees organic cotton grown without synthetic pesticides.

entries.microplastics: Zero microplastics. Cotton is a natural fiber that releases no synthetic particles in the wash.
entries.skinImpact: Breathes well, absorbs moisture, soft on skin. Ideal for sensitive skin types.
entries.envImpact: 100% biodegradable, but conventional farming uses a lot of water (2,000L per T-shirt). Better if organic.
entries.durability: 3–5 years with normal care. May shrink if washed at high temperatures.
entries.summary: The safe choice: no plastic, no irritation — just mind the origin of the cotton.

tips.title: Check the origin: BCI or GOTS?
tips.body: Not all cotton is equal. Look for GOTS (organic) or BCI (Better Cotton Initiative) certification on the label — these use up to 91% less water than conventional cotton.

tips.title: Wash at 30°C to last longer
tips.body: Cotton shrinks easily at high temperatures. Washing at 30°C extends the garment's life and uses much less energy.

tips.title: On next purchase, choose organic
tips.body: This garment is already a good choice. If you want to go further, next time choose GOTS-certified cotton: zero pesticides, traceable supply chain.

entries.microplastics: Zero microplastics. Pure natural fiber — no synthetic particles released during washing.
entries.skinImpact: No pesticide residues. Ideal for sensitive skin, children, underwear. The absolute best choice.
entries.envImpact: Grown without synthetic pesticides, uses up to 91% less water than conventional cotton. Biodegradable.
entries.durability: 4–6 years. The longer fibers of certified organic cotton tend to last longer.
entries.summary: The absolute top: natural, pesticide-free, biodegradable. If you can choose, choose this.

tips.title: You already made the best choice
tips.body: GOTS-certified organic cotton is the most sustainable clothing fiber available at scale. Keep it up.

tips.title: Recycle it at end of life
tips.body: When the garment is worn out, bring it to a fabric collection point or a store with textile take-back (H&M, Decathlon, Zara). Pure cotton is easy to recycle.

tips.title: Always wash at 30°C
tips.body: Even organic cotton benefits from low-temperature washing. Less energy, less wear: the garment lasts longer.

entries.microplastics: The 20% polyester releases approximately 150,000 microfibers per wash — less than other blends, but not zero.
entries.skinImpact: Cotton dominates: good breathability and comfort. Polyester adds shape retention and tear resistance.
entries.envImpact: Industrially unrecyclable blend, but the synthetic fraction is a minority. Intermediate impact.
entries.durability: 4–7 years. The blend is stronger than cotton alone and holds its shape better.
entries.summary: Acceptable: the majority is natural, but there is still a plastic fraction to consider.

tips.title: Laundry bag as a precaution
tips.body: 20% polyester is a small share, but still releases microfibers. A Guppyfriend Bag is a useful habit even for blends with low synthetic content.

tips.title: Aim for 100% natural
tips.body: For your next purchase, look for blends with an even higher natural percentage (90%+) or straight 100% organic cotton. The comfort won't change, the impact will.

tips.title: Not recyclable
tips.body: Like all cotton-synthetic blends, it cannot be industrially separated. Better to donate it than discard it when you stop using it.

entries.microplastics: The 5% elastane releases very few microfibers. Impact much lower than predominantly synthetic fabrics.
entries.skinImpact: Excellent comfort: cotton breathes and elastane adds stretch without chafing. Often used in jeans and stretch T-shirts.
entries.envImpact: Almost entirely natural. The small elastane fraction makes industrial recycling more complex, but the impact is contained.
entries.durability: 3–5 years. Elastane degrades before cotton, especially with frequent high-temperature washes.
entries.summary: Good choice: cotton dominates, elastane is minimal. Just mind washing at low temperatures.

tips.title: Wash at low temperature
tips.body: Elastane deteriorates quickly at high temperatures, losing elasticity. Wash at 30°C or less to preserve the fit over time.

tips.title: Look for organic cotton
tips.body: This blend is already valid. For your next choice, verify the cotton is GOTS or OEKO-TEX certified: same stretch, no pesticides.

tips.title: End of life: textile collection
tips.body: The small elastane percentage makes industrial recycling difficult. Bring it to a collection point instead of discarding — it can still be reprocessed.

entries.microplastics: Viscose is a semi-synthetic fiber. It releases some fibers during washing, but fewer than polyester.
entries.skinImpact: Soft and lightweight, good drape. But wrinkles easily, absorbs a lot of sweat and can cause irritation if low quality.
entries.envImpact: Made from wood cellulose using intensive chemical processes. Production uses toxic solvents. Biodegradable, but with a high production footprint.
entries.durability: 2–4 years. Wears out and deforms quickly, especially with frequent washing.
entries.summary: Looks natural (comes from wood) but the chemical process makes it semi-synthetic with significant environmental impact.

tips.title: Is it TENCEL or generic viscose?
tips.body: Conventional viscose uses toxic solvents. TENCEL Lyocell (Lenzing) uses a 99% closed-loop system with recovered solvents. Look for TENCEL on the label.

tips.title: Hand wash or delicate cycle
tips.body: Viscose loses shape and size easily in the washing machine. Hand wash or delicate programme at 30°C — low or no spin.

tips.title: Alternative: Lenzing Modal
tips.body: Modal produced by Lenzing (Austria) is a next-generation viscose: certified process, recycled solvents, biodegradable. Look for 'Lenzing Modal' or 'TENCEL Modal' on the label.

entries.microplastics: Zero microplastics. Linen is a completely natural plant fiber.
entries.skinImpact: Highly breathable, cool in summer, hypoallergenic. Softens over time, causes no irritation.
entries.envImpact: Flax plant: grows without pesticides, uses very little water (about 5x less than cotton), fully utilized. Excellent.
entries.durability: 10–20 years. One of the most resistant fibers in existence — linen garments last decades if cared for.
entries.summary: One of the world's most sustainable fabrics: no chemicals, minimal water, lasts a lifetime.

tips.title: Excellent choice already made
tips.body: Linen is among the fibers with the lowest environmental impact in the world. Pesticide-free farming, minimal water consumption, 100% biodegradable.

tips.title: Cold wash, avoid the dryer
tips.body: Linen shrinks at high temperatures. Wash at 30°C and air dry — it becomes softer with every wash.

tips.title: Lasts decades: care for it
tips.body: Unlike synthetics, quality linen improves with use and time. Store rolled, not folded, to avoid permanent creases.

entries.microplastics: Wool releases natural fibers during washing, not microplastics. Wool fibers are biodegradable.
entries.skinImpact: Excellent thermoregulation — warm in winter, cool in summer. May be slightly itchy on sensitive skin.
entries.envImpact: Natural, renewable and biodegradable animal fiber. Pay attention to animal welfare and pasture management.
entries.durability: 5–15 years. Quality wool lasts a long time, resists odors and needs few washes.
entries.summary: Excellent natural fiber with great durability: just mind the ethical origin from the producer.

tips.title: Look for RWS or ZQ certification
tips.body: RWS (Responsible Wool Standard) and ZQ Merino certify sheep welfare and sustainable pasture management. Look for these marks on the label.

tips.title: Wash rarely, air often
tips.body: Wool is naturally antibacterial — just air it outdoors to eliminate odors. Wash only every 5–10 uses, always cold with wool-specific detergent.

tips.title: End of life: compostable
tips.body: Pure wool is 100% compostable. If the garment is worn out and can't be donated, you can compost it or bring it to specialized textile collection.

entries.microplastics: Zero synthetic microplastics. Merino microfibers are 100% biodegradable.
entries.skinImpact: Extremely soft, no itch at all (unlike regular wool). Naturally antibacterial, regulates body temperature. Wearable directly on skin.
entries.envImpact: Natural and renewable. Certified merino wool (ZQ, RWS) guarantees sheep welfare and sustainable pasture management.
entries.durability: 7–20 years. Merino is among the most durable and long-lasting fibers. Holds shape well, rarely needs washing.
entries.summary: The elite of natural fabrics: soft as silk, thermoregulating, antibacterial, lasts decades.

tips.title: Absolutely the best choice
tips.body: Certified merino (ZQ or RWS) is the garment fabric with the best balance of performance, durability and environmental impact. No compromises.

tips.title: Wash less than you think
tips.body: Merino has natural antibacterial properties. After each use, air the garment instead of washing it. Recommended washing every 10–20 uses, cold water, gentle detergent.

tips.title: Long-term investment
tips.body: A quality merino garment lasts 10–20 years. The higher initial cost is fully amortized over time compared to synthetic garments that last 2–3 years.

entries.microplastics: The 50% polyester releases approximately 350,000 microfibers per wash. Intermediate but non-negligible impact.
entries.skinImpact: Balanced blend: cotton provides breathability, polyester provides dimensional stability. Widely used in budget sweatshirts.
entries.envImpact: Industrially unrecyclable. Half the fiber is from oil, half is natural — but the two cannot be separated.
entries.durability: 4–6 years. Resistant to deformation but accumulates odors over time.
entries.summary: The 50/50 sounds balanced but creates the same problem: plastic and cotton mixed cannot be recycled.

tips.title: Laundry bag mandatory
tips.body: With 50% polyester, every wash releases approximately 350,000 microfibers. Use a Guppyfriend Bag — it's the only practical way to reduce release.

tips.title: Towards more natural compositions
tips.body: For a similar next purchase, look for blends with at least 80% organic cotton. The fit is identical, the impact is much lower.

tips.title: Don't bin it: textile collection
tips.body: 50/50 is not industrially recyclable but can be reprocessed as insulating material. Always bring it to a textile collection point at end of life.

entries.microplastics: Modal is semi-synthetic (from beech wood) and releases some fibers during washing, fewer than virgin polyester.
entries.skinImpact: Extraordinarily soft — used in premium underwear. Cool, smooth, no chafing.
entries.envImpact: Modal (e.g. TENCEL Modal) can be produced in a closed loop with 99% recovered solvents. But not all modal is equal: ask for certification.
entries.durability: 3–5 years. Stays soft but tends to deform with heavy use.
entries.summary: Super soft and pleasant on skin, but always check if the modal is certified (Lenzing is the top).

tips.title: Is it Lenzing Modal?
tips.body: Not all modal is equal. Modal produced by Lenzing (Austria) uses a closed-loop process with 99% recovered solvents. Look for 'Lenzing Modal' or 'TENCEL Modal' on the label.

tips.title: Always delicate, never dryer
tips.body: Modal loses softness and shape at high temperatures. Wash at 30°C, delicate cycle, and air dry flat — never in the dryer.

tips.title: Superior alternative: TENCEL Lyocell
tips.body: TENCEL Lyocell has an even more efficient production process than modal with the same softness. For your next purchase, look for TENCEL (not just generic 'lyocell').

entries.microplastics: Typical activewear composition. Releases approximately 560,000 microfibers per wash — among the worst absolute levels.
entries.skinImpact: Compressive and form-fitting — useful for sport, but skin cannot breathe. With prolonged sweat can irritate and cause micro-abrasions.
entries.envImpact: Almost entirely petroleum-based synthetic, not biodegradable. Washing synthetic sportswear is the primary source of ocean microplastics.
entries.durability: 2–4 years. Elastane degrades first, losing elasticity especially with frequent washes.
entries.summary: Perfect for sport, terrible for the environment: use an anti-microplastic laundry bag and wash rarely.

tips.title: Guppyfriend Bag: non-negotiable
tips.body: Synthetic activewear is the #1 source of ocean microplastics. Every wash in a Guppyfriend Bag reduces release by up to 99% — for this type of garment it's essential.

tips.title: Rinse after sport, wash every 3 uses
tips.body: Rinse with cold water immediately after training to remove sweat. Machine wash only every 2–3 uses: fewer washes = fewer microfibers = garment lasts longer.

tips.title: Alternative: merino activewear
tips.body: For lower-intensity activities, ultra-light merino wool (150g/m²) competes with synthetic: thermoregulates, is antibacterial, releases no microplastics. Brands like Icebreaker or Smartwool.

entries.microplastics: Nylon releases approximately 500,000 microfibers per wash — similar to polyester but with typically larger particles.
entries.skinImpact: Non-breathable, generates static electricity, can cause irritation with prolonged use. But very resistant to tearing.
entries.envImpact: Nylon production emits nitrous oxide, a greenhouse gas 300 times more potent than CO₂. Not biodegradable.
entries.durability: 5–10 years. Mechanically very resistant — hence its use in backpacks, swimwear, technical socks.
entries.summary: Resistant but polluting: its production is among the most impactful in the textile sector.

tips.title: Wash rarely with laundry bag
tips.body: Nylon releases large microfibers particularly harmful to marine life. Always use a Guppyfriend Bag and wash as little as possible.

tips.title: Look for ECONYL (recycled nylon)
tips.body: ECONYL is regenerated nylon made from fishing nets and plastic waste recovered from the ocean. For your next nylon purchase (swimwear, tights, technical underwear), look for ECONYL on the label.

tips.title: Cold water only, never dryer
tips.body: Nylon deforms and yellows at high temperatures. Always wash cold (20–30°C) and air dry at room temperature.

entries.microplastics: The 65% polyester + viscose fraction release together approximately 500,000 fibers per wash. Among the worst blends.
entries.skinImpact: Viscose adds softness but doesn't compensate for polyester's poor breathability. Suitable for limited use, not all day.
entries.envImpact: Three different fibers, non-recyclable. Viscose production uses chemical solvents, polyester comes from petroleum.
entries.durability: 2–4 years. Tends to deform and become shiny in friction areas.
entries.summary: Three problematic fibers together: avoid if possible, especially for garments you wear often.

tips.title: Guppyfriend Bag mandatory
tips.body: This blend is among the worst for microfiber release. Never wash it without an anti-microplastic laundry bag. It's the only practical countermeasure you have.

tips.title: Wash rarely and cold
tips.body: Each wash also accelerates viscose deterioration. Airing the garment outdoors and using textile sanitizing sprays between washes extends its life.

tips.title: Next purchase: single fiber
tips.body: Three-component blends are the hardest to manage and dispose of. Choose single-fiber garments (100% organic cotton, 100% linen, 100% wool): easier to recycle, more durable, fewer microplastics.
